---
title: "Software"
description: >-
  Open-source research software by Natalia Gumińska: R packages for nanopore
  direct RNA sequencing, laboratory data infrastructure, and technical case
  studies.
toc: true
---

::: {.lead .reveal}
I write research software: R packages for nanopore direct RNA sequencing
analysis, and the relational databases that keep laboratory data usable. Most of
it is open source under the [LRB-IIMCB](https://github.com/LRB-IIMCB)
organisation, with personal projects on
[my GitHub](https://github.com/nemitheasura).
:::

## Packages and tools

::: {.card-grid}
::: {.u-card .reveal .d1}
### ninetails <span class="status-badge status-active">author</span>
Detection of **non-adenosine residues in poly(A) tails** from Oxford Nanopore
direct RNA reads. Raw current is segmented, converted to a two-dimensional
representation, and classified by a convolutional network — computer vision
applied to a signal problem. Author and maintainer. Published in *Nature
Communications* (2025), with a full pkgdown site and worked vignettes.

::: {.tag-row}
[R]{.pill} [Keras]{.pill} [Nanopore]{.pill} [Signal analysis]{.pill}
[Computer vision]{.pill}
:::

[Repository](https://github.com/LRB-IIMCB/ninetails){.btn-ghost}
[Documentation](https://lrb-iimcb.github.io/ninetails/){.btn-ghost}
[Paper](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-57787-6){.btn-ghost}
:::

::: {.u-card .reveal .d2}
### nanotail2 <span class="status-badge status-active">author</span>
R tooling for **poly(A) tail length analysis**: summarising distributions,
comparing conditions with appropriate statistics, and producing publication
figures from direct RNA sequencing output. Pairs with ninetails so that length
and composition can be reported from one pipeline.

::: {.tag-row}
[R]{.pill} [tidyverse]{.pill} [Statistics]{.pill} [Visualisation]{.pill}
:::

[Organisation](https://github.com/LRB-IIMCB){.btn-ghost}
:::

::: {.u-card .reveal .d3}
### NanoQuRe <span class="status-badge status-maintained">contributor</span>
Quality control for nanopore sequencing runs. Turns raw run metrics into a
readable report, so a bad flow cell surfaces before three days of analysis, not
after.

::: {.tag-row}
[R]{.pill} [Quality control]{.pill} [Reporting]{.pill}
:::

[Repository](https://github.com/LRB-IIMCB/NanoQuRe){.btn-ghost}
:::

::: {.u-card .reveal .d1}
### rDNAmine <span class="status-badge status-active">co-author</span>
Analysis of **long repetitive rDNA sequences** in Oxford Nanopore reads — the
regions short-read assemblers collapse. Co-authored with A. Czarnocka-Cieciura
and described in *Yeast* (2026).

::: {.tag-row}
[Shell]{.pill} [Long reads]{.pill} [rDNA]{.pill}
:::

[Repository](https://github.com/LRB-IIMCB/rDNA_mine){.btn-ghost}
[Paper](https://doi.org/10.1002/yea.70023){.btn-ghost}
:::

::: {.u-card .reveal .d2}
### lachesis <span class="status-badge status-maintained">contributor</span>
Part of the laboratory's ecosystem of tools supporting nanopore RNA analysis
workflows. Contributor.

::: {.tag-row}
[R]{.pill} [Workflow]{.pill}
:::

[Organisation](https://github.com/LRB-IIMCB){.btn-ghost}
:::

::: {.u-card .reveal .d3}
### Laboratory databases <span class="status-badge status-maintained">maintained</span>
Relational **SQL databases** designed for the laboratory: samples, libraries,
sequencing runs and their metadata, with role-based access and an audit trail.
Records export to analysis-ready YAML, so a pipeline run can state exactly which
sample it consumed.

::: {.tag-row}
[MySQL]{.pill} [PHP]{.pill} [Schema design]{.pill} [Lab data]{.pill}
:::
:::
:::

## Technical case studies

Three problems worth writing down, because the interesting part was not the
code.

::: {.u-card .reveal}
### Turning a one-dimensional signal into a classification problem

Poly(A) tails are read as a long, nearly featureless run of ionic current. A
single non-adenosine residue perturbs it slightly, and the perturbation is
smaller than the run-to-run variation between flow cells. Threshold rules on the
raw trace either miss most events or drown in false positives.

**Approach.** Segment the tail region, normalise per read, project the segment
into a two-dimensional representation, and train a convolutional classifier on
synthetic tails with known composition. The framing change — signal problem to
image problem — is what made the accuracy acceptable.

**Trade-off.** A neural network is harder to justify to reviewers than a
threshold. The answer was to ship the training data, the model, and a vignette
that reproduces the reported numbers, so the claim is checkable rather than
trusted.

::: {.tag-row}
[ninetails]{.pill} [R]{.pill} [Keras]{.pill}
:::
:::

::: {.u-card .reveal}
### Making a sequencing laboratory's metadata queryable

Sample provenance lived in spreadsheets, one per person, with no shared
vocabulary for something as basic as a kit version. Answering "which runs used
RNA004?" meant asking five people.

**Approach.** A normalised MySQL schema covering samples, libraries, runs and
instruments, administered through phpMyAdmin so the lab did not need a new tool
to learn, with a thin PHP/PDO front end for entry and an audit table recording
every change.

**Trade-off.** Normalisation costs data-entry convenience. Controlled
vocabularies for kits and instruments were worth the friction; free-text notes
fields were kept deliberately, because the alternative is people encoding notes
into sample names.

::: {.tag-row}
[MySQL]{.pill} [PHP]{.pill} [Audit logging]{.pill}
:::
:::

::: {.u-card .reveal}
### Supporting two chemistries without forking the pipeline

Direct RNA reads arrive 3′→5′ with the tail anchored to a known adapter. cDNA
reads arrive in either orientation and need the tail found relative to a primer.
The biology question is the same; the signal handling is not.

**Approach.** A shared core with chemistry-specific front ends: orientation
detection and anchoring differ, tail extraction and classification do not.
Regression tests pin both paths to fixed expected output, so a change to the
shared core cannot silently improve one and break the other.

**Trade-off.** Two entry points is more surface area to document than one
auto-detecting function. It is also honest about the fact that the two cases
genuinely behave differently, which an auto-detector would hide.

::: {.tag-row}
[R]{.pill} [Dorado]{.pill} [testthat]{.pill}
:::
:::

## How I build

::: {.highlight-box .reveal}
Documented, tested, version controlled. Packages ship with vignettes and worked
examples, so a colleague can run an analysis on their own data without booking a
meeting with me first. Function interfaces are treated as promises: they change
on a deprecation cycle, not on a whim.
:::

::: {.btn-row}
<a class="btn-cta" href="stack.qmd"><i class="bi bi-stack"></i> Full stack</a>
<a class="btn-ghost" href="side-quests.qmd"><i class="bi bi-compass"></i> Side quests</a>
<a class="btn-ghost" href="https://github.com/nemitheasura"><i class="bi bi-github"></i> GitHub</a>
:::
